title: Listening - Part 3: The Future of News
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a tech expert, Sonia, and a veteran journalist, Abbi, discussing the impact of AI on newsrooms. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview exploring the tension between automated reporting and investigative integrity.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Journalism has survived the printing press and the internet, but can it survive the algorithm? I’m joined by Sonia, a tech developer, and Abbi, a senior editor. Sonia, we’re already seeing AI write sports recaps and financial reports. Is this the end for human journalists?

Sonia: [15] Not the end, but a radical job description change. AI excels at what we call 'commodity news'—data-heavy, formulaic stories that follow a predictable pattern. By automating these, we actually liberate human journalists from the drudgery of data entry. [/15] It allows them to focus on the 'why' and the 'how' rather than just the 'what.' The danger isn't that AI replaces quality journalism, but that publishers use it as an excuse to cut costs rather than reinvest in investigative depth.

Interviewer: Abbi, as an editor, do you see AI as a helpful tool or a threat to credibility?

Abbi: [16] It’s a double-edged sword. AI can help us sift through millions of leaked documents in seconds, identifying patterns a human might miss for years. However, journalism is built on trust and human sources. [/16] An algorithm can’t walk into a bar and convince a whistleblower to share a secret. It doesn't have a moral compass. If we rely too much on automated content, we risk a 'race to the bottom' where the news becomes a generic, hallucinated echo chamber without accountability.

Interviewer: Sonia, what about the 'echo chamber' effect? Does AI make it harder for us to see the whole picture?

Sonia: [17] Algorithms are designed to give you what you want, not what you need to hear. If you only click on sensationalist headlines, the machine will bury the nuanced, complex policy stories. [/17] We’re moving toward a 'fragmented truth' where two people can live in the same city but read two completely different versions of reality based on their digital footprints. This is where human editors are vital—to act as the gatekeepers of shared facts.

Interviewer: Abbi, some people say the 'journalist' is becoming an 'editor of machines.' Is that accurate?

Abbi: [18] It’s a depressing thought, but there’s truth in it. We spend more time fact-checking AI-generated drafts than we do writing from scratch. The real skill of the future is 'verification.' [/18] In a world of deepfakes and automated propaganda, a journalist’s value will be their ability to say: 'I was there, I saw this with my own eyes, and I can prove it’s real.' The physical presence of a reporter is becoming a premium product.

Interviewer: Sonia, is there a risk that AI will eventually have its own political bias?

Sonia: [19] AI is just a mirror of its training data. If the data it 'eats' is biased, the output will be biased. The problem is that the bias is often invisible, buried in layers of code that even the developers don't fully understand. [/19] We need 'algorithmic transparency'—we need to know who trained the model and what their objectives were. Without that, we are just blindly trusting a black box.

Interviewer: Abbi, final thoughts. Is there room for optimism?

Abbi: [20] Yes, because I think the public is developing 'algorithm fatigue.' People are starting to crave long-form, deeply researched stories that only a human brain—with all its empathy and intuition—can produce. We might see a return to a 'slow news' movement, where quality matters more than clicks. [/20]

Interviewer: Sonia, Abbi, thank you both.

questions.question: Sonia argues that the automation of 'commodity news' is positive because
options: A) it produces fewer factual errors than human writing.
B) it forces publishers to hire more investigative reporters.
C) it allows journalists to dedicate more time to analytical work.
D) it reduces the cost of newspapers for the general public.
questions.explanation: She states that automating data-heavy stories 'liberates' journalists to focus on the 'why' and 'how'.

questions.question: What is Abbi's main concern about the limitations of AI in journalism?
options: A) It is too slow at processing large amounts of leaked data.
B) It cannot build the personal relationships needed for whistleblowing.
C) It is too expensive for small newsrooms to implement.
D) It often gets distracted by irrelevant social media trends.
questions.explanation: She notes that 'an algorithm can't walk into a bar and convince a whistleblower' and lacks a moral compass.

questions.question: Sonia suggests that algorithms tend to
options: A) prioritize complex policy stories over sensationalism.
B) hide factual information from users who have not paid a subscription.
C) reinforce a user's existing preferences rather than challenging them.
D) confuse people by showing too many different viewpoints at once.
questions.explanation: She says algorithms give you what you want, not what you need, burying nuanced stories if you only click sensationalism.

questions.question: According to Abbi, the most important skill for future journalists will be
options: A) learning how to code their own AI models.
B) writing as quickly as an automated system.
C) confirming the authenticity of information and sources.
D) finding ways to make news more entertaining for young people.
questions.explanation: She says the real skill is 'verification' and being able to prove that something is real in a world of deepfakes.

questions.question: Why does Sonia believe that 'algorithmic transparency' is necessary?
options: A) to allow other companies to copy successful AI models
B) to ensure that the developers are paid fairly for their work
C) to reveal the hidden biases that may be present in the code
D) to make the software easier for journalists to use.
questions.explanation: She argues that bias is often invisible and buried in code, so we need to know who trained the model and why.

questions.question: Abbi's 'slow news' prediction is based on the idea that
options: A) people are becoming tired of mass-produced, automated content.
B) governments will soon pass laws to limit the speed of the internet.
C) investigative stories are actually cheaper to produce than AI stories.
D) journalists will refuse to work for fast-paced digital outlets.
questions.explanation: She mentions 'algorithm fatigue' and people craving long-form stories that only humans can produce.
